description: Nature, landscapes, plants, and wild animals

items.notes: Pronounced 'MET-sä'. Finland is about 75% forest, so this is an essential word. Partitive: metsää.
examples: Metsä on kaunis. — The forest is beautiful.
Kävelen metsässä. — I walk in the forest.

items.notes: Pronounced 'YÄR-vi'. Finland is called 'the land of a thousand lakes' (actually 188,000!). Partitive: järveä.
examples: Järvi on syvä. — The lake is deep.
Suomessa on paljon järviä. — There are many lakes in Finland.

items.notes: Pronounced 'YO-ki'. Partitive: jokea. Changes stem in some forms: joen (genitive).
examples: Joki virtaa hitaasti. — The river flows slowly.
Joen rannalla on kaunista. — It's beautiful on the riverbank.

items.notes: Pronounced 'ME-ri'. Partitive: merta. Itämeri = Baltic Sea (literally 'East Sea').
examples: Meri on tyyni. — The sea is calm.
Asun meren lähellä. — I live near the sea.

items.notes: Pronounced 'VUO-ri'. The 'uo' is a diphthong. Finland has very few mountains; they are called 'tunturi' in Lapland. Partitive: vuorta.
examples: Vuori on korkea. — The mountain is high.
Kiipeämme vuorelle. — We climb the mountain.

items.notes: Pronounced 'SAA-ri'. Double 'a' is long. Finland has thousands of islands in its archipelago. Partitive: saarta.
examples: Saari on pieni. — The island is small.
Veneellä pääsee saareen. — You can get to the island by boat.

items.notes: Pronounced 'RAN-ta'. Partitive: rantaa. Can mean lakeshore, seashore, or beach.
examples: Ranta on hiekkainen. — The beach is sandy.
Istumme rannalla. — We sit on the shore.

items.notes: Pronounced 'NIIT-ty'. Double 'i' and double 't' are both long. Partitive: niittyä.
examples: Niityllä kasvaa kukkia. — Flowers grow in the meadow.
Niitty on vihreä kesällä. — The meadow is green in summer.

items.notes: Pronounced 'SUO'. The 'uo' diphthong. Finland has extensive bog areas. Partitive: suota.
examples: Suo on märkä. — The swamp is wet.
Suomessa on paljon soita. — There are many swamps in Finland.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KAL-li-o'. Double 'l' is long. Refers to bedrock or a rock formation, not a small stone (which is 'kivi'). Partitive: kalliota.
examples: Kallio on jyrkkä. — The cliff is steep.
Istun kalliolla. — I sit on the rock.

items.notes: Pronounced 'PUU'. Double 'u' is long. Means both 'tree' and 'wood' (material). Partitive: puuta.
examples: Puu on vanha. — The tree is old.
Talo on puuta. — The house is made of wood.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KUK-ka'. Double 'k' is long. Partitive: kukkaa. Plural: kukat.
examples: Kukka tuoksuu hyvältä. — The flower smells good.
Ostin kukkia. — I bought flowers.

items.notes: Pronounced 'RUO-ho'. The 'uo' diphthong. Partitive: ruohoa.
examples: Ruoho on vihreää. — The grass is green.
Istun ruoholla. — I sit on the grass.

items.notes: Pronounced 'LEH-ti'. Also means 'newspaper' or 'magazine'. Partitive: lehteä. Plural: lehdet.
examples: Lehdet putoavat syksyllä. — The leaves fall in autumn.
Lehti on keltainen. — The leaf is yellow.

items.notes: Pronounced 'OK-sa'. Partitive: oksaa. Used in sauna whisks (vihta/vasta) made from birch branches.
examples: Lintu istuu oksalla. — The bird sits on a branch.
Oksa katkesi. — The branch broke.

items.notes: Pronounced 'SAM-mal'. Double 'm' is long. Very common in Finnish forests. Partitive: sammalta.
examples: Sammal on pehmeää. — Moss is soft.
Kivet ovat sammalen peitossa. — The rocks are covered in moss.

items.notes: Pronounced 'SIE-ni'. The 'ie' diphthong. Mushroom picking is a beloved Finnish tradition. Partitive: sientä.
examples: Keräämme sieniä metsästä. — We pick mushrooms from the forest.
Tämä sieni on myrkyllinen. — This mushroom is poisonous.

items.notes: Pronounced 'MAR-ja'. Partitive: marjaa. Berry picking (marjanpoiminta) is a Finnish summer tradition. Everyman's rights allow picking in any forest.
examples: Poimin marjoja. — I pick berries.
Mustikka on suosittu marja. — Blueberry is a popular berry.

items.notes: Pronounced 'LIN-tu'. Partitive: lintua. Finland has many migratory bird species.
examples: Lintu laulaa. — The bird sings.
Näin kauniin linnun. — I saw a beautiful bird.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KA-la'. Partitive: kalaa. Fishing is very popular in Finland. Also a common food.
examples: Kala ui järvessä. — The fish swims in the lake.
Syön kalaa usein. — I eat fish often.

items.notes: Pronounced 'HIR-vi'. Partitive: hirveä. Very common in Finland; watch for moose road signs! 'Hirveä' also means 'terrible' (unrelated).
examples: Hirvi ylitti tien. — The moose crossed the road.
Suomessa on paljon hirviä. — There are many moose in Finland.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KAR-hu'. Partitive: karhua. The brown bear is Finland's national animal. Finns traditionally avoided saying the bear's real name (otso, kontio) out of respect.
examples: Karhu nukkuu talvella. — The bear sleeps in winter.
Karhu on Suomen kansalliseläin. — The bear is Finland's national animal.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KET-tu'. Double 't' is long. Partitive: kettua. Red foxes are common in Finland.
examples: Kettu on ovela. — The fox is cunning.
Näin ketun metsässä. — I saw a fox in the forest.

items.notes: Pronounced 'YÄ-nis'. Note: jänis is a hare (wild), not a pet rabbit (kani). Partitive: jänistä.
examples: Jänis juoksi pellolle. — The hare ran to the field.
Jäniksen turkki muuttuu talvella valkoiseksi. — The hare's fur turns white in winter.

items.notes: Pronounced 'O-ra-va'. Partitive: oravaa. Red squirrels are common in Finnish forests and parks.
examples: Orava kiipeää puuhun. — The squirrel climbs the tree.
Orava kerää pähkinöitä. — The squirrel collects nuts.
